title: Observable — How DNA Copies Itself
logline: The double helix unzips and templates two copies — leading strand continuous, lagging strand in Okazaki fragments. Semi-conservative replication, proven by Meselson–Stahl; B-DNA geometry from PDB 1BNA.

brand.title: Observable
brand.subtitle: Upon Tyne Science — evidence before wonder
brand.cta: Follow Observable — one measurement at a time.

science_subject.key_measurement: B-DNA helical geometry from X-ray crystallography (PDB 1BNA, Drew et al. 1981); semi-conservative replication demonstrated by Meselson & Stahl (1958); proofreading drives error rates to ~1 in 10^9–10^10 bases
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific molecular visualization, DNA replication fork — right-handed antiparallel B-DNA parent duplex (geometry per PDB 1BNA) unwound at a Y-shaped fork, DNA polymerase on a ring-shaped sliding clamp building a continuous leading strand and a discontinuous lagging strand of Okazaki fragments, single-strand binding proteins on exposed strands, crowded aqueous nucleoplasm, neutral dark background, accurate relative scale, no anthropomorphic faces, no straight-line directed motion, time-compression implied, Jantzen molecular-cellular fidelity, 16:9, ILLUSTRATIVE NOT TO SCALE label lower corner

sources.citation: Drew HR, Wing RM, Takano T, Broka C, Dickerson RE. Structure of a B-DNA dodecamer: conformation and dynamics. PNAS. 1981;78(4):2179-2183 (PDB 1BNA)
sources.notes: Canonical right-handed antiparallel B-DNA geometry — CC0 coordinates

sources.citation: Meselson M, Stahl FW. The replication of DNA in Escherichia coli. PNAS. 1958;44(7):671-682
Alberts B, et al. Molecular Biology of the Cell, 7th ed., Ch. 5 — DNA Replication (leading/lagging strand, Okazaki fragments)
sources.notes: Established textbook consensus for fork topology

beats.speech_text: Every time one of your cells divides, it copies three billion letters of DNA — and it almost never makes a mistake. How?

The double helix splits down the middle, and each old strand becomes a template for a new one — so every copy keeps one original strand. That's called semi-conservative. But the two strands run in opposite directions, so only one is copied smoothly; the other is built backwards in short pieces called Okazaki fragments, then stitched together.

We're not guessing. In nineteen fifty-eight, Meselson and Stahl tracked DNA by weight and proved the semi-conservative pattern. And the helix itself was pinned down atom by atom with X-ray crystallography — the B-DNA coordinates are public.

Here is the fork: a right-handed helix unwinding, one strand copied continuously, the other in fragments. This is a structural model, sped up and not to scale — and it's driven by random molecular motion and chemical energy, not a machine marching with purpose.
beats.visualization_prompt: DNA replication fork, right-handed antiparallel B-DNA per PDB 1BNA, leading continuous strand and lagging Okazaki fragments, single-strand binding proteins, crowded nucleoplasm, no faces, Jantzen molecular-cellular fidelity

beats.speech_text: This copying is why you grow, heal, and inherit traits — and the rare error that slips past proofreading is the raw material of both disease and evolution. Precision and imperfection, in the same machinery.

lines: EVIDENCE: B-DNA geometry PDB 1BNA (Drew/Dickerson 1981); semi-conservative replication (Meselson & Stahl 1958)
VIZ: Illustrative structural model — labeled NOT TO SCALE; time-compressed; Jantzen molecular fidelity
